slides.title:
Simplifying Remote
Physical Therapy
slides.description: WizeCare brings physical therapy into the patient's home. We redesigned the core clinical workflows — patient management, care plan editing, and protocol creation — to reduce friction and drive adoption.

slides.label: The Context
slides.title: Clinicians who don't have time to learn new software
slides.content: Physiotherapists using WizeCare manage packed clinic days and rely on manual routines built over years. They're quick to abandon tools that add steps instead of removing them.
slides.highlight: For WizeCare to succeed, the system needed to feel like a time-saver — not an extra task.

slides.label: The Problem
slides.title: Three places the experience broke down
slides.description: We visited clinics, ran interviews, and reviewed support tickets. The same friction appeared in the same three places every time.

issues.title: Patient screen unclear
issues.description: Clinicians couldn't find care plans or patient details — no hierarchy, no predictable navigation

issues.title: Care plan editing too complex
issues.description: Editing required navigating multiple screens — context lost at every step

issues.title: No protocol creation flow
issues.description: No way to build from scratch — rigid templates didn't fit complex cases

slides.label: User Research
slides.title: What clinicians told us
slides.content: Across every clinic we visited, the same frustrations came up — not as complaints, but as things clinicians had simply accepted.
slides.highlight: These weren't edge cases — they were accepted parts of the daily routine.

image.caption: Field research — clinic visits and user interviews

slides.label: Design Goals
slides.title: What we set out to fix.

goals.title: Simplify navigation
goals.description: Patient data and key actions reachable without hunting.

goals.title: Streamline care plan editing
goals.description: One focused flow instead of multiple disconnected screens.

goals.title: Enable protocol creation
goals.description: Let experienced clinicians build and customise from scratch.

goals.title: Reduce the learning curve
goals.description: Self-explanatory for new and experienced users alike.

slides.title: Patient Management
slides.subtitle: The first place clinicians land — and where they spent most of their time feeling lost.

slides.label: Patient Screen
slides.title: Everything a clinician needs — in one place

beforeImage.caption: Old — cluttered tabs, buried adherence data

afterImage.caption: New — dedicated patient page, clear hierarchy

slides.beforeDescription: Cluttered tabs, no clear hierarchy, and adherence metrics buried where no one could find them.
slides.afterDescription: One dedicated page — patient info, active plans, and progress clearly separated.
beforeBullets: Oversized tabs with no clear hierarchy
No dedicated patient page — plans hard to locate
Adherence metrics buried — most useful, hardest to find
afterBullets: All patient information in one predictable place
Clear separation between info, plans, and progress
Adherence metrics surfaced as the primary clinical signal
slides.highlight: Clinicians went from searching across screens to finding everything in one predictable place.

slides.title: Care Plan Editing
slides.subtitle: The most frequent task in the platform — and the one with the most unnecessary steps.

slides.label: Care Plan Editing
slides.title: Editing a plan shouldn't require a map

beforeImage.caption: Old — multi-screen flow, dense exercise lists

afterImage.caption: New — single screen, exercise cards, inline editing

slides.beforeDescription: Multiple screens to complete a single edit — exercises in dense lists, no patient data in view.
slides.afterDescription: Everything on one screen. Each exercise as its own card. Patient data pinned throughout.
beforeBullets: 4–5 screens per edit — context lost every step
Dense exercise lists — easy to misread at scale
No patient health data visible while editing
afterBullets: Single screen for the full editing flow
Each exercise on its own card — easy to compare and reorder
Patient data pinned for constant clinical context
slides.highlight: Irrelevant information removed entirely — the screen shows only what clinicians need to act.

slides.title: Protocol Builder
slides.subtitle: Experienced physiotherapists want to build their own protocols — the old system gave them no way to do it.

slides.label: Research Insight
slides.quote: Physiotherapists prefer to create their own protocols rather than rely on predefined care plans. Each case requires specific adjustments — rigid templates just don't fit how they work.
slides.author: Clinic interviews

slides.label: Protocol Builder
slides.title: From rigid templates to full clinical control

beforeImage.caption: Old — no creation flow, rigid templates only

slides.beforeDescription: No protocol creation flow — experts navigated a multi-step system with no way to build from scratch.
slides.afterDescription: A Kanban builder with drag-and-drop gives clinicians full control — built on the same card system as the care plan editor.
beforeBullets: No dedicated creation flow — no starting point for experts
Rigid templates didn't fit complex or custom cases
afterBullets: Drag-and-drop Kanban — exercise library left, board right
Same card system as care plan editor — zero additional learning
Full control over structure, order, and grouping
slides.highlight: Reusing the card system across both flows meant the new builder felt immediately familiar.

slides.label: Outcomes
slides.title: Less friction. More time for patients.

outcomes.title: Saved per patient session
outcomes.description: Less time navigating — more time on clinical decisions.

outcomes.title: Misclicks and prescription errors
outcomes.description: Clearer layouts and inline editing reduced mistakes during care plan editing.

outcomes.title: Daily active usage
outcomes.description: Clinicians who had avoided the platform started using it as their primary tool.

outcomes.title: Dependence on support and training
outcomes.description: A self-explanatory system meant clinicians could onboard and work independently.

slides.title: Thank You
slides.subtitle: Designing systems that respect the people who use them.
